title: Treacle soda bread
subtitle: Dark, slightly sweet Irish soda bread with black treacle
excerpt: A quick, dense Irish soda bread made with black treacle for colour and a malty, slightly bitter sweetness. The leavening is entirely bicarbonate of soda activated by buttermilk; there is no yeast and no rise time. Mixed in under 10 minutes and baked straight away, it produces a thick, rough-textured loaf with a tender, close crumb that suits butter and sharp cheddar or smoked fish.
sourceNotes: Soda bread using bicarbonate of soda activated by buttermilk has been documented in Ireland from the early nineteenth century, following the commercial availability of bread soda. Florence Irwin, The Cookin' Woman (1949, now public domain under UK copyright duration) describes a range of Irish soda breads including brown and treacle versions. The treacle addition is a standard variation recorded in Irish domestic economy and cookery guides published before 1928.

content.text: The key rule with soda bread is speed: once the buttermilk meets the bicarbonate of soda, the leavening reaction starts immediately. Mix quickly, shape with minimal handling, and get the loaf into the oven within 5 minutes of mixing. The
deep cross cut in the top
before baking is not optional: it allows heat into the centre and is the reason soda bread cooks through without the outside burning.

Heat the oven to 200°C conventional (180°C fan). Line a baking tray with baking paper. Mix the two flours, bicarbonate of soda, and salt in a large bowl. Warm the treacle briefly so it pours easily, then stir it into the buttermilk. Pour the liquid into the flour mixture and stir quickly with a fork until just combined. The dough should be shaggy and sticky; do not overmix.

Tip onto a floured surface and pat quickly into a round about 5 cm thick. Place on the lined tray. Cut a cross about three-quarters of the way through the loaf. Sprinkle with rolled oats if using. Bake for 40 to 45 minutes until the loaf sounds hollow when tapped on the base. Cool for at least 15 minutes on a rack before cutting; cutting too soon gives a gummy interior.
